notes: First AfroKitchen recipe expansion wave. Recipes are synthesized from multiple references and common household patterns, not copied from a single source.

recipes.name: Moi Moi
recipes.description: A steamed Nigerian bean pudding made from peeled black-eyed peas blended with peppers, onion, oil, and seasoning, then cooked until set and tender.
recipes.story: Moi Moi is one of Nigeria's classic bean dishes, often served beside jollof rice, fried rice, pap, garri, or bread. A good batter is smooth, lightly peppery, and soft after steaming, with optional egg, fish, corned beef, or crayfish folded in when the table calls for it.
recipes.occasion: Best for Sunday rice plates, parties, breakfast spreads, and make-ahead meal prep.
recipes.best_served_with: Jollof rice, fried rice, pap, custard, garri, or soft bread.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use banana leaves for aroma, some use ramekins or foil bags. Add-ins vary by household, with boiled egg, fish, corned beef, and crayfish all common.
recipes.review_summary: The AfroKitchen version follows the common Nigerian pattern: peeled black-eyed or brown beans, pepper, onion, oil, seasoning, optional crayfish or egg, and steaming in leaves, tins, ramekins, or foil until set.
recommended_changes: Keep boiled egg and crayfish optional because sources treat them as common add-ins, not mandatory.
Do not over-thicken the batter. Multiple references rely on enough water to steam into a soft pudding.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian culinary references and encyclopedia support.

sources.title: All Nigerian Recipes - Tastiest Nigerian Moi Moi Recipe
All Nigerian Recipes - Prepare Nigerian Moi Moi with 5 Ingredients
Yummy Medley - Moi Moi Nigerian Bean Pudding

ingredients.name: warm water
ingredients.prep_note: plus more as needed for blending

steps.title: Soak and peel the beans
steps.instruction: Soak the beans until the skins loosen, then rub and rinse until most skins are removed. Drain well.
steps.tip: Pre-peeled beans save time, but whole beans give the most control over texture.

steps.title: Blend the batter
steps.instruction: Blend peeled beans with bell pepper, scotch bonnet, onion, oil, nutmeg, crayfish if using, and enough warm water to make a smooth pourable batter.
steps.tip: The batter should pour like thick pancake batter, not sit like paste.

steps.title: Season and fill
steps.instruction: Season with salt, fold in optional egg pieces, and pour into leaves, ramekins, tins, or foil cups. Leave headroom for expansion.

steps.title: Steam until set
steps.instruction: Set the cups in a pot with a little simmering water, cover tightly, and steam until the pudding is firm through the center.
steps.tip: Top up hot water around the cups if the pot dries, but keep water out of the batter.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Let the Moi Moi rest briefly so it firms up, then serve warm or at room temperature.

recipes.name: Efo Riro
recipes.description: A Yoruba spinach and pepper stew cooked with palm oil, locust beans, crayfish, and assorted meats or fish.
recipes.story: Efo Riro is a rich Yoruba vegetable soup where greens are folded into a pepper base enriched with palm oil, iru, crayfish, and cooked proteins. The magic is to cook the pepper base until it tastes deep, then fold in the greens briefly so they stay lively.
recipes.occasion: Best for family meals, swallow plates, and rice bowls.
recipes.best_served_with: Amala, eba, pounded yam, fufu, boiled rice, or plantain.
recipes.regional_variations: Amaranth, efo shoko, efo tete, or spinach may be used depending on market access. Protein mixes vary widely.
recipes.review_summary: The common references agree on leafy greens, pepper blend, palm oil, locust beans or iru, crayfish, onion, and assorted meat or fish. Spinach is a practical diaspora substitute for Nigerian greens.
recommended_changes: Keep iru and crayfish visible because they define the classic Yoruba flavor.
Use spinach as an available substitute but name amaranth or efo shoko as the traditional green.
recipes.official_source_status: No official Nigerian government recipe page was found in this batch pass. Sources include regional food references, encyclopedia context, and diaspora reporting.

sources.title: Epicurious - Nigerian recipes, Efo Riro with Kemi Seriki
supports: dried shrimp or crayfish flavor profile

steps.title: Blend and reduce the peppers
steps.instruction: Blend bell peppers, scotch bonnet, and half the onion into a coarse pepper base. Simmer until some water cooks off.
steps.tip: A reduced base keeps the soup rich instead of watery.

steps.title: Fry the aromatics
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil gently, add sliced onion and iru, then fry until fragrant. Stir in the pepper base and crayfish.

steps.title: Add the proteins
steps.instruction: Add cooked meat and smoked fish. Simmer so the pepper sauce coats the proteins and the oil rises slightly.

steps.title: Fold in the greens
steps.instruction: Add squeezed greens in batches, stirring until just wilted and coated. Taste and season with salt.
steps.tip: Do not cook the greens to mush. Efo Riro should still have body.

recipes.name: Nigerian Pepper Soup
recipes.description: A hot, aromatic Nigerian broth made with goat, chicken, fish, or assorted meat, pepper soup spice, chile, onion, and scent leaves.
recipes.story: Pepper soup is a flexible Nigerian broth served in homes, bars, and gatherings. It should be hot, aromatic, and clear enough to sip, with meat or fish, ehu or pepper soup spice, chile heat, onion, and fresh herbs like scent leaf or uziza.
recipes.occasion: Best for cold evenings, celebrations, recovery meals, and small chops menus.
recipes.best_served_with: White rice, boiled yam, agidi, or served alone as a hot broth.
recipes.regional_variations: Goat meat, chicken, catfish, assorted beef, cow foot, and yam versions are common. Scent leaf is preferred, while uziza or parsley may appear as substitutes.
recipes.review_summary: The recipe follows the common Nigerian pepper soup format: meat or fish simmered with onion, pepper, ehu or pepper soup spice, scent leaf or uziza, and enough broth to serve hot.
recommended_changes: Keep protein flexible because sources show goat, chicken, catfish, cow foot, and assorted meat variants.
Name ehu or calabash nutmeg because several Nigerian sources identify it as a defining spice.
recipes.official_source_status: No official Nigerian government recipe page was found in this batch pass. The reference set is regional food writing and encyclopedia context.

sources.title: All Nigerian Recipes - Nigerian Pepper Soup
All Nigerian Recipes - Nigerian Goat Meat Pepper Soup

ingredients.name: pepper soup spice or ground ehu

steps.title: Start the meat
steps.instruction: Place meat in a pot with quartered onion, salt, and enough water to cover. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a steady simmer.
steps.tip: Goat and cow foot take longer than chicken or fish. Top up water as needed.

steps.title: Season the broth
steps.instruction: Add pepper soup spice or ground ehu, scotch bonnet, thyme if using, and sliced onion. Simmer until the broth smells aromatic.

steps.title: Finish with herbs
steps.instruction: Stir in scent leaves or uziza and simmer briefly. Taste for salt and pepper.

steps.title: Serve hot
steps.instruction: Serve immediately as a broth, or ladle over boiled yam, rice, or agidi.

recipes.name: Puff Puff
recipes.description: Soft Nigerian fried dough balls made from yeasted flour batter, lightly sweetened and fried until golden.
recipes.story: Puff Puff is a beloved Nigerian snack and small-chops staple. The common version is a simple yeasted batter of flour, sugar, salt, warm water, and sometimes nutmeg, fried by the spoonful until round and golden.
recipes.occasion: Best for parties, breakfast snacks, tea time, and street-food platters.
recipes.best_served_with: Tea, zobo, pap, or a spicy pepper dip.
recipes.regional_variations: Similar fried dough snacks appear across West Africa under names such as bofrot, kala, or mikate, with small changes in spice and texture.
recipes.review_summary: The recipe follows the widely repeated puff puff profile: flour, yeast, sugar, salt, warm water, optional nutmeg, one rise, then deep frying until golden.
recommended_changes: Keep nutmeg optional because it is common but not universal.
Use a 60 minute rise as a practical midpoint across sources.
recipes.official_source_status: No official Nigerian government recipe page was found in this batch pass. The references are culinary sources and encyclopedia context.

sources.title: K's Cuisine - Nigerian Puff Puff Recipe
The Guardian - Puff puff Nigerian doughnuts recipe

steps.title: Mix the batter
steps.instruction: Combine flour, sugar, yeast, salt, and nutmeg if using. Stir in warm water until the batter is smooth, stretchy, and thicker than pancake batter.

steps.title: Let it rise
steps.instruction: Cover the bowl and leave in a warm place until the batter is bubbly and roughly doubled.

steps.title: Fry in batches
steps.instruction: Heat oil over medium heat. Scoop batter into the oil with wet fingers or a spoon and fry, turning often, until deep golden.
steps.tip: If the outside browns before the center cooks, lower the heat.

steps.title: Drain and serve
steps.instruction: Drain on paper towels and serve warm.

recipes.name: Waakye
recipes.description: A Ghanaian rice and beans dish cooked with dried sorghum or millet leaves for its signature reddish-brown color.
recipes.story: Waakye is one of Ghana's great rice-and-beans dishes, sold from street carts and cooked at home. The signature color comes from sorghum or millet leaves, with baking soda sometimes used as a substitute when leaves are unavailable.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, lunch packs, street-food plates, and weekend family meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Shito, tomato stew, gari, spaghetti, boiled egg, fried plantain, salad, fish, beef, or chicken.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use red beans rather than black-eyed peas. Accompaniments vary from simple shito and egg to fully loaded street plates.
recipes.review_summary: The core profile is stable across sources: rice and beans cooked together with sorghum or millet leaves for color, then served with shito, stew, eggs, plantain, fish, meat, salad, or spaghetti.
recommended_changes: Keep sorghum or millet leaves as the preferred ingredient and baking soda as fallback.
Describe accompaniments as optional because the rice-and-beans base is the recipe.
recipes.official_source_status: A Ghana Revenue Authority newsletter result mentions waakye, but this batch did not find an official recipe standard. The dish is verified through food references and sorghum industry documentation.

ingredients.name: baking soda
ingredients.substitution: use only if waakye leaves are unavailable

steps.title: Color the cooking water
steps.instruction: Rinse the sorghum leaves and simmer them in water until the water turns reddish-brown.

steps.title: Cook the beans
steps.instruction: Add black-eyed peas to the colored water and simmer until nearly tender.

steps.title: Add rice
steps.instruction: Remove the leaves, stir in rice and salt, cover tightly, and cook on low until the rice is tender and the beans are cooked through.

steps.title: Rest and serve
steps.instruction: Let the pot rest covered, then fluff gently and serve with shito, stew, eggs, plantain, or protein.

recipes.name: Red Red
recipes.description: A Ghanaian black-eyed pea stew cooked with red palm oil and served with fried ripe plantain.
recipes.story: Red Red gets its name from the red palm oil and the red-orange fried plantain often served beside the beans. The stew should be hearty and glossy, with black-eyed peas carrying onion, tomato, chile, garlic, and ginger.
recipes.occasion: Best for lunch, vegetarian plates, street-food style meals, and hearty weekday dinners.
recipes.best_served_with: Fried ripe plantain, gari, rice, avocado, or boiled egg.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks add fish powder, smoked fish, or shrimp powder, while vegan versions keep the stew bean-forward.
recipes.review_summary: Sources consistently frame red red as a Ghanaian black-eyed pea stew cooked with palm oil or vegetable oil and served with fried plantain.
recommended_changes: Keep palm oil because it explains the dish's name and color.
Treat fish powder or smoked fish as optional, since vegetarian red red is also common.
recipes.official_source_status: No official Ghanaian recipe standard was found in this batch pass. The dish is confirmed by culinary references and encyclopedia context.

sources.title: McCormick for Chefs - Red Red with Fried Plantains
Forward Food - Ghanaian Red Red Stew

steps.title: Cook the beans
steps.instruction: Simmer black-eyed peas in water until tender but not collapsed. Reserve a little cooking liquid, then drain.

steps.title: Build the stew base
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil, then cook onion, garlic, ginger, scotch bonnet, tomatoes, and tomato paste until the sauce thickens.

steps.title: Stew the beans
steps.instruction: Stir beans into the sauce with a splash of reserved cooking liquid. Simmer until glossy and well coated. Season with salt.

steps.title: Fry the plantain
steps.instruction: Fry ripe plantain slices in oil until caramelized and golden. Serve beside the beans.

recipes.name: Banku and Tilapia
recipes.description: A Ghanaian plate of smooth fermented corn and cassava dough served with seasoned grilled tilapia and pepper sauce.
recipes.story: Banku is a Ghanaian staple made by cooking fermented corn and cassava dough into a smooth, elastic mound. Paired with grilled tilapia and pepper sauce, it becomes one of Ghana's most recognizable restaurant and roadside plates.
recipes.occasion: Best for weekend grilling, coastal-style meals, and Ghanaian restaurant plates.
recipes.best_served_with: Shito, fresh pepper sauce, sliced onion, tomatoes, and cucumber.
recipes.regional_variations: Banku may be served with okra stew, soup, pepper sauce, grilled fish, or fried fish. The tilapia seasoning varies by cook.
recipes.review_summary: The recipe follows the common Ghanaian plate: banku made from fermented corn and cassava dough, served with grilled or fried tilapia and pepper sauce.
recommended_changes: Keep the banku itself simple: corn dough, cassava dough, water, and salt.
Treat the tilapia spice mix as flexible because source recipes vary by cook.
recipes.official_source_status: No official Ghanaian recipe standard was found in this batch pass. The recipe is supported by Ghanaian and regional culinary sources.

sources.title: Wikipedia - Banku
supports: served with fish and pepper sauce

sources.title: Guardian Nigeria - Ghana Traditional Dish, Banku And Tilapia
supports: banku and tilapia as Ghanaian dish

sources.title: Burnaby Neighbourhood House - Banku and Fried Tilapia PDF

steps.title: Season the fish
steps.instruction: Rub scored tilapia with ginger, garlic, onion, scotch bonnet if using, oil, and salt. Let it sit while you start the banku.

steps.title: Start the banku
steps.instruction: Whisk corn dough, cassava dough, water, and salt in a heavy pot until smooth. Cook over medium heat, stirring constantly.

steps.title: Steam the banku
steps.instruction: When the dough thickens, reduce heat, splash in a little water, cover briefly, then stir again until smooth and elastic.
steps.tip: A strong wooden spoon helps. Banku should be smooth, not grainy.

steps.title: Grill the tilapia
steps.instruction: Grill or broil the tilapia, turning once, until cooked through and lightly charred.

steps.title: Plate
steps.instruction: Shape banku into balls and serve with grilled tilapia, shito or pepper sauce, and sliced vegetables.

recipes.name: Githeri
recipes.description: A Kenyan maize and beans dish, often simmered plain or finished with onion, tomato, and vegetables.
recipes.ethnic_group: Gikuyu, Meru, Mbeere, Embu and wider Kenya
recipes.story: Githeri is a Kenyan staple built on boiled maize and beans. Many homes eat it simply salted, while contemporary versions fry onion, tomato, vegetables, and spices around the cooked maize and beans.
recipes.occasion: Best for weekday meals, school-style bowls, and batch cooking.
recipes.best_served_with: Kachumbari, avocado, sukuma wiki, or a little chile sauce.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions remain plain maize and beans; others add potatoes, carrots, greens, curry powder, or tomato.
recipes.review_summary: The core dish is maize and beans boiled together. A light onion-tomato finish is a common modern approach, while the simple boiled version remains valid.
recommended_changes: Keep maize and beans as the non-negotiable core.
Mark tomatoes, carrots, and spices as a modern finish rather than the base identity.
recipes.official_source_status: Kenya's Recipe Book of Common Mixed Dishes includes githeri. Additional accessible references confirm the maize-and-beans core.

ingredients.name: beans
ingredients.prep_note: kidney beans or rose coco, soaked if dry

steps.title: Boil maize and beans
steps.instruction: Simmer maize and beans in water until both are tender. Add more water if needed and season with salt near the end.
steps.tip: Dry maize and dry beans can take much longer. Pre-cooked maize and beans shorten the recipe.

steps.title: Make the tomato finish
steps.instruction: Heat oil, then fry onion until soft. Add tomatoes, carrots if using, and curry powder if using. Cook until saucy.

steps.title: Combine
steps.instruction: Add drained maize and beans to the tomato finish. Stir with a splash of cooking liquid and simmer until flavors come together.

steps.title: Serve
steps.instruction: Finish with coriander if using and serve hot with kachumbari or avocado.

recipes.name: Mukimo
recipes.description: A Kenyan mashed potato dish with maize, peas or beans, and green vegetables, often served with stew or nyama choma.
recipes.story: Mukimo is a comforting Kenyan mash associated especially with central Kenya. Potatoes give it body, maize brings chew, peas or beans add sweetness, and pumpkin leaves, spinach, or amaranth give the mash its green lift.
recipes.occasion: Best for family meals, ceremonies, roast meat plates, and stew dinners.
recipes.best_served_with: Beef stew, nyama choma, chicken stew, kachumbari, or sauteed greens.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use peas, others beans. Pumpkin leaves are traditional, while spinach is a practical substitute.
recipes.review_summary: The common pattern is potatoes mashed with maize, peas or beans, and greens. Onion or butter is optional, while pumpkin leaves or spinach provide the green color.
recommended_changes: Keep pumpkin leaves as traditional and spinach as an accessible substitute.
Do not over-blend. Mukimo can be smooth but should still feel like a mash.
recipes.official_source_status: Kenya's Recipe Book of Common Mixed Dishes includes mukimo. Additional Kenyan and humanitarian sources support the same ingredient pattern.

sources.title: Go Visit Kenya - Kenya Mukimo Recipe
World Vision - In the kitchen, Mukimo recipe

steps.title: Boil potatoes and peas
steps.instruction: Boil potatoes and peas in salted water until the potatoes are tender.

steps.title: Cook the greens
steps.instruction: Add chopped pumpkin leaves or spinach and cook until tender and bright green.

steps.title: Mash
steps.instruction: Drain excess water, add boiled maize, then mash until combined. Leave some maize kernels whole for texture.

steps.title: Finish
steps.instruction: If using onion, fry it in butter or oil and fold into the mash. Taste for salt and serve hot.

recipes.name: Malva Pudding
recipes.description: A warm South African baked pudding flavored with apricot jam, then soaked with a buttery cream sauce.
recipes.story: Malva pudding is a South African favorite with a soft sponge, apricot jam, vinegar and bicarbonate lift, and a hot cream sauce poured over the pudding as it comes from the oven.
recipes.occasion: Best for Sunday lunch, winter dessert, braai desserts, and holiday tables.
recipes.best_served_with: Custard, vanilla ice cream, or whipped cream.
recipes.regional_variations: Some recipes use evaporated milk, cream, or milk in the sauce. The apricot jam and hot soak are the important shared features.
recipes.review_summary: Sources consistently identify the South African dessert as an apricot-jam sponge leavened with bicarbonate and vinegar, then soaked with warm butter, sugar, and cream or milk sauce.
recommended_changes: Keep apricot jam, vinegar, and bicarbonate because they show up as defining ingredients.
Pour the sauce over the pudding while hot so it absorbs properly.
recipes.official_source_status: No official South African government recipe standard was found in this batch pass. The recipe is supported by encyclopedia, food media, and hospitality recipe references.

sources.title: Salty Ginger - Traditional South African Malva Pudding

steps.title: Make the sponge batter
steps.instruction: Beat egg and sugar until pale. Mix in apricot jam, melted butter, and vinegar. Fold in flour, bicarbonate of soda, salt, and milk.

steps.title: Bake
steps.instruction: Pour into a greased baking dish and bake at 180 C until risen and golden.

steps.title: Make the sauce
steps.instruction: Warm cream, sugar, butter, and hot water until the sugar dissolves and the sauce is smooth.

steps.title: Soak the pudding
steps.instruction: Pierce the hot pudding with a skewer or fork and pour the warm sauce over it gradually so it absorbs.
steps.tip: Serve warm, not cold, for the classic soft texture.
